fact: December 22 is almost always the winter solstice in the Northern Hemisphere, which means it has the shortest day and longest night of the entire year!
While kids in North America are bundling up for winter, children in Australia are getting ready for their longest day of summer sunshine on December 22!
Arctic foxes are at their fluffiest around this date because their winter coats are fully grown - they're like walking snowballs with legs!
In northern Alaska and parts of Canada, the sun doesn't rise at all on December 22, creating a spooky twilight that lasts all day long.
Pine trees and other evergreens look extra green against the snow around this time because they're pumping special antifreeze chemicals through their needles to survive the cold.
December 22 falls right in the middle of Capricorn season, making it the goat's time to shine in the zodiac world.
Hibernating animals like bears and groundhogs are in their deepest sleep around this date - their heart rates can drop to just 8 beats per minute!
The moon looks extra bright and sparkly around December 22 because cold winter air is clearer and has less dust and moisture to block the light.
In places like Norway and northern Canada, the aurora borealis (northern lights) are most active around the winter solstice, painting the sky in green and purple swirls.
Owls are super active around this date because the long winter nights give them extra hunting time to catch mice and other small creatures.
In the Southern Hemisphere, December 22 marks the beginning of their hottest season, when penguins in Antarctica actually get too warm and have to find shade!
